params.text:
Ordering, storage and disposal

Medicines must be handled securely from the moment they arrive until they are safely disposed of. In a care home the home is responsible for ordering; in the community, arrangements are agreed with the person and recorded in their care plan.

  • Storage — keep medicines secure, at the right temperature, and accessible only to authorised staff.
  • Fridge medicines — some medicines, such as certain insulins and eye drops, must be kept in a medicines fridge. Care homes monitor the fridge temperature; a person's own home fridge does not need daily checks.
  • Controlled drug (CD) cabinets — controlled drugs must be stored in a specific, secured CD cabinet.
  • Disposal — return unwanted medicines to the pharmacy; controlled drugs and sharps need special disposal arrangements.

params.question: Which of the following are handled correctly? (Select all that apply.)

answers.text: Storing a medicine that requires refrigeration in a designated medicines fridge.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Fridge-line medicines must be kept cold in a suitable fridge to remain effective and safe.

answers.text: Keeping controlled drugs in a locked, dedicated CD cabinet.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Controlled drugs require secure storage in a specific CD cabinet.

answers.text: Returning unwanted and out-of-date medicines to the pharmacy for disposal.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Medicines should be returned to the pharmacy; they must never go in normal waste or down the sink.

answers.text: Storing a person's medicines loose in an unlocked kitchen drawer with easy access for anyone.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Medicines must be stored securely and accessible only to those who should have them.
